art.title: foodmasku
art.medium: Performance, Photography, Video, NFT and Food
list: Began April 2020 during COVID lockdown, after a faulty Zoom pickle-face filter inspired the artist to create a kale salad mask.
"foodmasku" is a bilingual pun — "food + mask + u" in English; in Bahasa Indonesia/Javanese it reads as "food of my brother"
Named by The New York Times as one of five Instagram art accounts to follow (2020)
"Farm-to-Face" was commissioned for the UN SDG Action Campaign's UNITE TO ACT digital exhibition and unveiled at the 2023 UN SDG Action Awards, on the margins of the UN Food Systems Summit

The "Dining In" performance — two guests eating a meal off the artist's face — went viral multiple times across TikTok and Instagram, accumulating tens of millions of views and later featured on MTV's Ridiculousness
exhibitions: SXSW, NFT Gallery, Austin, TX — Livestream in Your Head (live performance with William Cobbing) — 2022
The Invisible Dog Art Center, Brooklyn, NY — Nafas (Dining In performance) — September 10 – October 15, 2022
Galerie Iham, Paris — What We Eat, Who We Are (solo show, Mangemasku series) — May 16, 2023
UN SDG Action Awards, UN Food Systems Summit — Farm-to-Face (UNITE TO ACT digital exhibition) — 2023
Essex Art Center, Lawrence, MA — Salon Show 2024 (group show, curated by Mariana Martins; featuring Good Luck Has Arrived, 2023) — July 13 – August 10, 2024
ArtVerse Gallery, Paris — All & Every (duo show with Tommy Hollenstein, presented during the Paris Olympics and Paralympics) — July 25 – September 8, 2024

press.title: Why this N.Y. artist is spending the pandemic turning his meals into face masks
The Instagram-Favorite Artist Who Makes Masks Out of Food Is the Star of a New Show About the Intersection of Art and Cuisine
Q&A: Foodmasku on channeling emotions into food mask art
From Farm to Face: How Foodmasku's Art Unites Creativity and Sustainable Food Systems
The internet thought allowing people to eat off your face was a job

art.description:
foodmasku is an ongoing performance, photography, and video series in which I make meals into masks and then eat them. It began in April 2020, during the early weeks of COVID lockdown, when a colleague's stuck pickle filter on a Zoom call prompted me to put kale on my face. It was the first I'd heard laughter in weeks. I felt it was important to find joy through art again. And so, a daily art practice was born.

Over the years the project has grown into a body of work at the intersection of trauma, food waste, and responsible consumption. The UN commissioned me to make a series on sustainable food systems, using the face as a site for meditating on how food reaches our tables, and what we choose to do with it when it arrives. Farm-to-Face was unveiled at the 2023 UN SDG Action Awards alongside the UN Food Systems Summit.

The series has also lived an unruly second life online. A performance staged at The Invisible Dog Art Center where two guests quietly eating dinner off my face was cut, recaptioned, and recirculated across TikTok and Instagram, racking up tens of millions of views and a segment on MTV's Ridiculousness. The runaway clip is frequently paired with fabricated captions about a "restaurant paying $10,000 a month for rich people to eat off your face," a viral rumor I continue to publicly debunk. The real piece is about labor, food inequality, and who gets to be seen at the table.

art.title: Post Papa
exhibitions: Essex Art Center, Lawrence, MA — Post Papa (on view) — through September 13, 2025
art.description:
Post Papa is a sketch series that I drew on Post-its while sitting beside my father in hospice.

The work was exhibited at Essex Art Center in Lawrence, MA through September 13, 2025, with half of all proceeds supporting the center, a non-profit arts community in the Merrimack Valley.

art.title: Since the Shooting
list: On July 5, 2013, I was shot by a stranger on a Brooklyn street while walking to the subway from my apartment in Bedford-Stuyvesant. The bullet was meant for someone else; it passed between two ribs, narrowly missed my heart, and lodged in my stomach.

Three strangers kept me alive until the ambulance arrived — a local barber who applied pressure to the wound, a woman from the neighborhood who kept me awake by yelling at me, and a man on his way to a job interview who fanned me with his résumé.
I spent four days in a sedated coma at Kings County Hospital, followed by seven months of physical therapy.

I was the first person Kathy Shorr photographed for her series SHOT 101 Survivors of Gun Violence in America, and have worked with Mayors Against Illegal Guns (now Everytown for Gun Safety), Moms Demand Action, Gays Against Guns, Teachers Unify to End Gun Violence, and many other organizations on gun-violence prevention.

press.title: Gunshot Victim Takes A Selfie A Day To Document His Healing
This is what gun violence looks like, for those who survive
Bed-Stuy Shooting Victim Meets the Man Who Saved His Life
101 Unflinching Portraits of Survivors of US Gun Violence

art.description:
Since the Shooting was an ongoing photographic practice of daily self-portraits featuring scars that run across my body from a bullet to the chest. It began the day I was released from Kings County Hospital in July 2013 and continued until the day of my shooter's trial.

On July 5, 2013 — the morning after the Fourth of July — a stranger came out of a passing car to shoot ata pregnant woman on my street in Bedford-Stuyvesant. The woman was shot in the leg, and the bullet that would have gone through her head hit me instead. The bullet went through me, between two ribs, narrowly missed my heart, and lodged itself in my stomach. I spent four days in a sedated coma and seven months in physical therapy. Three strangers kept me alive until the ambulance came: a barber who applied pressure to the wound, a neighbor who kept me awake by yelling at me, and a man on his way to a job interview who fanned me with his résumé.

The series began as a way to show my healing, one photograph a day, as a slow act of witnessing recovery from trauma. I carried the camera with me into hospital visits, onto planes, and into every apartment of every city I moved to. The scar became a reason to keep going. I continued the daily practice from the day I left the hospital through the day of my shooter's trial, and have continued making work in and around the series in the years since. I was also the first person photographed by Kathy Shorr for SHOT: 101 Survivors of Gun Violence in America.

art.title: If We Were XYZ
list: Performance by Melati Suryodarmo
Sound and vocal composition by Jessika Kenney
EEG data, custom sleep-lab instrumentation, and sonification / visualization by Antonius Oki Wiriadjaja
Commissioned by Asia Society Museum for Creative Common Ground
exhibitions: Asia Society Museum, New York — If We Were XYZ (premiere, commissioned by Creative Common Ground) — October 18–19, 2019

press.title: A Performer Uses Brain-Mapping Technology to Scan Her Dreams
If We Were XYZ: Artistic Process

art.description:
If We Were XYZ is a three-hour durational performance by Indonesian artist Melati Suryodarmo, premiered at Asia Society Museum in October 2019 as part of the museum's interdisciplinary program Creative Common Ground. I served as the piece's artist-technologist: I built a self-devised sleep laboratory around Melati, recording EEG data while she slept, and then translated that raw neural data into the musical scores and visual systems that structured the performance. Vocalist and composer Jessika Kenney used the same dataset to write the piece's seven-part score — one movement for each dream.

The work sits at the intersection of Javanese mysticism, neuroscience, and Freudian psychology, asking what it means when the subconscious is made material. It was, in many ways, a question about intimacy: what do you owe a collaborator who has handed you the raw signal of their dreaming mind, and what kind of translation is faithful to that trust?
